methodologies.name: Auction-Based Task Allocation
methodologies.summary: Hand out tasks to a group of self-interested agents by running an auction that rewards honesty. The auction is built so that bidding your true value is the best move you can make. Two common forms are the second-price sealed-bid auction (Vickrey) for single items and a generalised version (VCG) for bundles. Because honesty pays, the auction learns each agent's real value instead of a strategic bluff, and the result spreads the tasks where they are worth the most. This is more than the generic contract-net pattern. It is the step-by-step procedure for choosing the auction form, setting the payment rule, and proving that honesty really is the best move for your setting.
methodologies.intent: Choose and set up an auction that rewards honest bidding, so self-interested agents reveal their true values and the tasks go where they are worth the most.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when scarce tasks or resources must be shared among several self-interested agents, and you cannot assume they will report their true value under a naive rule. Don't apply it when agents fully cooperate, because then the honesty machinery is overhead you do not need. One exception: even cooperative groups sometimes use auction mechanics to break ties or surface preferences cheaply.

inputs.name: Bidder population
inputs.description: The agents that will bid. Each one privately knows how much the goods are worth to it.

inputs.name: Goods or tasks to allocate
inputs.description: The items being auctioned. These can be single goods, bundles, or task contracts with deliverables.

inputs.name: Social objective
inputs.description: What a good outcome means here. Usually it means putting tasks where they are worth the most. Sometimes it means raising the most revenue, or meeting a fairness rule.

outputs.name: Auction mechanism
outputs.description: The chosen auction form, such as Vickrey, VCG, English, Dutch, or first-price sealed-bid. It includes the bid format, how winners are picked, and the payment rule.

outputs.name: Truthfulness proof or argument
outputs.description: A demonstration that honest bidding is the best move under the chosen auction. Or a clear note about where that breaks down.

outputs.name: Allocation and payments
outputs.description: For each round, which goods go to which agents and what each agent owes.

diagram.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
  participant A as Auctioneer
  participant B1 as Bidder 1
  participant B2 as Bidder 2
  participant BN as Bidder N
  Note over A: announce goods, mechanism, payment rule
  A->>B1: call for sealed bids
  A->>B2: call for sealed bids
  A->>BN: call for sealed bids
  B1-->>A: bid b1
  B2-->>A: bid b2
  BN-->>A: bid bN
  Note over A: winner determination (sort or VCG optimisation)
  A->>A: compute payment rule (Vickrey: 2nd-price; VCG: externality)
  A-->>B1: allocation + payment
  A-->>B2: allocation + payment
  A-->>BN: allocation + payment
  Note over A: log bids + computation for audit

methodologies.example_scenario:
A cloud-cost team at a media company runs an internal 'spot capacity' market. Every hour, idle GPU minutes on the rendering cluster go to the data-science teams whose training jobs are queued. Teams value that capacity very differently. One team's quarterly model training is worth far more than another's exploratory notebook. A naive 'first-come-first-served' rule rewarded teams that gamed the queue by submitting placeholder jobs early.

Applying the methodology, the cost team modelled each team's payoff as its value for the capacity minus its internal chargeback (a quasilinear model). For single-GPU-hour slots they used a second-price sealed-bid auction (Vickrey). Honest bidding became the best move, and the placeholder-gaming stopped overnight. For bundled training runs that needed N consecutive GPU-hours, they switched to the bundle version (VCG). They capped the winner-picking computation at 30 seconds and wrote down the resulting approximation gap. The payment rule ran as a small service. They checked the auction's assumptions. Bids were sealed behind an API, not posted in a Slack thread. Collusion was kept in check by the small number of bidders and an audit log. Budgets were generous enough not to distort bidding.

What they learned: the audit log caught one broken assumption in month two. Two teams had quietly agreed to take turns bidding high in alternate weeks. The auction's correctness did not stop this collusion. But the audit surfaced it within 48 hours of the first incident. They added a soft policy and made bid distributions visible to leadership. Measured by the company-wide chargeback model, the new market improved allocation efficiency by 17% over the naive baseline.
methodologies.deep_dive:
This is mechanism design under **quasilinear preferences** (utility_i = v_i(outcome) − payment_i), per Leyton-Brown & Shoham's treatment of mechanism design and auctions.

- **Single-good case — the Vickrey auction**: a second-price sealed-bid auction (Vickrey 1961). Allocating to the highest bid and charging the *second-highest* bid makes truthful bidding a **dominant strategy** (dominant-strategy incentive-compatible, DSIC / strategy-proof): a bidder can never improve its outcome by misreporting, regardless of others' bids. The allocation is efficient (welfare-maximising).
- **Combinatorial case — VCG**: the **Vickrey–Clarke–Groves** mechanism generalises the property to bundles. Each winner pays the **externality** it imposes — the loss in others' total welfare caused by its participation (the difference between others' optimal welfare without it and with it). VCG is DSIC and allocatively efficient over the full combinatorial domain.
- **Why the payment rule is load-bearing**: strategy-proofness is a property of the *pricing*, not just the allocation. Charge first-price (pay-your-bid) instead and truthfulness collapses — first-price and ascending English auctions are not DSIC.
- **Winner determination**: trivial sort for a single good; the combinatorial allocation problem is **NP-hard** in the worst case, so deployments cap solve time and accept a bounded approximation gap (which also weakens the exact incentive guarantee).
- **Assumption surface**: the DSIC theorems assume sealed bids, no collusion, no binding budget constraints below equilibrium, and (for Vickrey) single-dimensional private values. Real deployments violate these at the edges — collusion and repeated-game side-deals are the canonical leak — so correctness is only verifiable *ex post* via an audit log of bids, winner-determination runs, and payments.

steps.name: Model each bidder's value and payoff
steps.description: Model each bidder's payoff as its value for the goods minus what it pays (a quasilinear model). Write down what bidders can value: a single item, a bundle, or a value for each possible outcome. The auction form depends on this shape.

steps.name: Pick an auction form that rewards honesty
steps.description: For a single item, the second-price sealed-bid auction (Vickrey) makes honest bidding the best move. For bundles, the VCG version extends that property. First-price and English auctions do not reward honesty. Pick those only when simplicity or balanced payments matter more than honest bidding.

steps.name: Define how winners are picked
steps.description: Specify how winners are computed from the bids. For a single item this is just a sort. For bundles it is a hard optimisation that can blow up in the worst case. Acknowledge that cost and cap it with an approximation if needed.

steps.name: Specify the payment rule
steps.description: For Vickrey, the winner pays the second-highest bid. For VCG, each winner pays for the harm it does to everyone else by taking part. Get this rule wrong and the honesty incentive disappears.

steps.name: Check the assumptions the proof rests on
steps.description: Confirm the deployment meets what the proof needs: sealed bids, no collusion, no tight budget limits, and single-dimensional values for Vickrey. Where an assumption fails, write down the strategic risk that remains.

steps.name: Instrument and audit
steps.description: Log every bid, every winner computation, and every payment. You can only confirm the auction behaved correctly after the fact. Without logs you cannot catch manipulation or drift.

principles: Pick the auction that makes honest bidding the best move, then prove it holds for your setting.
The payment rule is not decoration. It is what makes honesty pay.
Picking winners can be slow. For bundles, use an approximation and write down the gap.
Honesty incentives rest on assumptions, such as sealed bids and no collusion. Name where your deployment breaks them.

known_failure_modes.note: Bidders discover an assumption violation (collusion, side-payments, repeated-game dynamics) that the mechanism's truthfulness proof did not cover.
Combinatorial winner-determination in VCG without a computational cap — the allocation step never terminates on hard instances.

sources.title: Multiagent Systems (2nd edition, ed. Gerhard Weiss, MIT Press 2013)
sources.chapter: Ch 7 'Mechanism Design and Auctions' — Kevin Leyton-Brown and Yoav Shoham — §1 Introduction; §2 Mechanism Design with Unrestricted Preferences; §3 Quasilinear Preferences; §4 Efficient Mechanisms; §5 Single-Good Auctions; §6 Position Auctions; §7 Combinatorial Auctions; §8 Conclusions
sources.quote: Mechanism Design and Auctions — Kevin Leyton-Brown and Yoav Shoham — 1 Introduction, 2 Mechanism Design with Unrestricted Preferences, 3 Quasilinear Preferences, 4 Efficient Mechanisms, 5 Single-Good Auctions, 6 Position Auctions, 7 Combinatorial Auctions, 8 Conclusions

sources.title: Counterspeculation, Auctions, and Competitive Sealed Tenders (William Vickrey, 1961)
sources.quote: Vickrey, W. (1961). Counterspeculation, Auctions, and Competitive Sealed Tenders. The Journal of Finance, 16(1), 8-37. DOI: 10.1111/j.1540-6261.1961.tb02789.x — the foundational second-price-sealed-bid auction paper underlying VCG mechanism design.

methodologies.name: Voting-Based Group Decision
methodologies.summary: Turn many agents' differing preferences into one group decision by choosing a voting rule that fits what the group needs. Different rules guarantee different things, such as picking a clear pairwise winner, rewarding honest votes, or dividing things fairly. The methodology is the procedure for choosing the rule, collecting votes in the right form, and admitting the limits no rule can escape (the Arrow and Gibbard-Satterthwaite results). It is more than just defaulting to majority vote. The rule choice is itself the design decision, and different rules can pick different winners from the very same votes.
methodologies.intent: Pick a voting rule whose guarantees fit the group decision, then combine the agents' votes under that rule while being clear about its limits.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when several agents hold different preferences over a fixed set of options and one group choice must be made. Examples are ranking tasks, choosing among options, voting on a config, or settling a multi-agent debate. Don't apply it when one agent's preference is authoritative; just defer to it. Skip it when preferences cannot be put into words; a numeric score may be more honest than a forced ranking. One exception: even with a single agent, a voting rule can combine several internal critics or model votes.

inputs.name: Agent preferences
inputs.description: Each agent's preference over the options. This may be a ranking, a score, an approval set, or pairwise comparisons, depending on how you collect it.

inputs.name: Alternative set
inputs.description: The fixed set of options being voted on, such as candidates, plans, tasks, or configurations.

inputs.name: Desired axiomatic properties
inputs.description: Which guarantees matter to you, ranked by what the deployment cannot live without. Examples include picking a clear pairwise winner, never punishing a candidate for gaining support, and resisting manipulation.

outputs.name: Selected voting rule
outputs.description: The chosen rule, such as plurality, Borda count, approval voting, a Condorcet method, STV, or Copeland. The choice is justified by the guarantees you wanted.

outputs.name: Group decision
outputs.description: The single option, or the ranking, the rule produces from the agents' votes.

outputs.name: Impossibility caveat
outputs.description: A clear note of which guarantees the chosen rule gives up. Arrow's result proves you cannot have them all at once.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  prefs[Agent preferences + alternative set] --> rank[Rank desired axiomatic properties]
  rank --> elicit[Elicit in format the rule consumes]
  elicit --> pick{Pick voting rule}
  pick -- top-only --> plur[Plurality]
  pick -- full ranking --> borda[Borda count]
  pick -- pairwise winner --> cond[Condorcet method e.g. Copeland or Schulze]
  pick -- approval set --> appr[Approval voting]
  pick -- proportional --> stv[STV]
  plur --> tie[Specify tie-break]
  borda --> tie
  cond --> tie
  appr --> tie
  stv --> tie
  tie --> agg[Run aggregation on ballots]
  agg --> caveat[Document Gibbard-Satterthwaite manipulation surface]
  caveat --> audit[Audit ballots + outcome]
  audit --> out[Group decision + impossibility caveat]

methodologies.example_scenario:
A platform group at a fintech runs a quarterly 'engineering priorities' vote across seven feature teams. The default had been plurality, where each team picks one favourite from the roadmap items. The winner was usually a splitting choice that nobody hated but few teams strongly wanted. After two quarters of dissatisfaction, the platform lead ran the voting-based group-decision methodology.

They ranked the guarantees they cared about. They wanted to never pick an option when a unanimously better one exists. They wanted to pick the clear pairwise winner when one exists. And they wanted resistance to obvious manipulation. Plurality failed the pairwise-winner test badly on their past votes. Borda count failed it in one out of three replays, and the legal team flagged it as gameable by adding near-duplicate candidates. They picked the Schulze method, a rule that elects the pairwise winner and has a documented tie-break by path strength. For collection, each team submitted a full ranking of the 12 candidate items through a small internal app. The tie-break and manipulation caveats went into the announcement.

What they learned: re-running past votes showed Schulze would have elected a different winner in two of the prior three quarters. After the fact, the teams preferred both of those winners. The first live Schulze election produced a clear pairwise winner. They documented the manipulation exposure honestly, since every non-dictatorial rule can be gamed somewhere (Gibbard-Satterthwaite). They named two specific manipulation patterns to watch for in audits. Different rules really do pick different winners from the same votes. Making the rule choice explicit moved the conversation from 'who won' to 'are we choosing well.'
methodologies.deep_dive:
This is computational social choice — preference aggregation via a voting rule selected against its axiomatic profile (Brandt, Conitzer & Endriss).

- **The hard ceiling (Arrow's Impossibility Theorem)**: for ≥3 alternatives, no social welfare function jointly satisfies Unrestricted Domain, Pareto efficiency, Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives, and Non-Dictatorship (Arrow 1951/1963). You must give one up — so rank the properties and accept the documented loss.
- **The manipulation ceiling (Gibbard–Satterthwaite)**: every non-dictatorial, onto voting rule over ≥3 alternatives is **manipulable** — some preference profile exists where a voter benefits from misreporting. There is no strategy-proof escape; the design task is to identify *which* manipulations your deployment is exposed to, not to eliminate them.
- **Rule property profiles**:
  - **Plurality**: top-choice only; fails **Condorcet consistency** (can fail to elect a candidate who beats all others pairwise) and suffers vote-splitting.
  - **Borda count**: uses full rankings; Condorcet-inconsistent and vulnerable to **clone candidates** and strategic burying.
  - **Condorcet methods (Copeland, Schulze)**: elect the **Condorcet winner** (pairwise-beats-all) when one exists. A **Condorcet cycle** (the Condorcet paradox) can leave no such winner, so the tie-break (Schulze: strongest path strength) is part of the rule, not an afterthought.
  - **Approval voting**: reduces strategic complexity; **STV**: gives proportionality.
- **Other axioms in play**: monotonicity (more support never hurts a candidate), anonymity (voter symmetry), neutrality (candidate symmetry), Pareto.
- **Verification is ex post**: log every ballot and rule application and re-run offline — different rules genuinely elect different winners on identical ballots, so the rule choice must be auditable.

steps.name: Collect preferences in the right form
steps.description: Rankings, scores, approval sets, and pairwise comparisons are not interchangeable. Collect votes in the form the chosen rule needs. If collection cost is the main concern, decide the form before you pick the rule.

steps.name: Rank the guarantees you need
steps.description: List the guarantees that matter, such as electing a clear pairwise winner, never punishing a candidate for gaining support, resisting manipulation, treating voters and candidates evenly, and respecting unanimity. Arrow's result says no rule can give all of them for three or more options. So rank yours and accept the losses you can live with.

steps.name: Pick a rule whose guarantees match
steps.description: Plurality is simple but ignores everything below each voter's top choice. Borda count uses full rankings but is easy to game. Condorcet methods, such as Copeland and Schulze, elect the pairwise winner when one exists. Approval voting reduces strategic guessing. STV gives proportional results.

steps.name: Run the count
steps.description: Apply the rule to the collected ballots. Some rules can hit a cycle where no clear winner exists (the Condorcet paradox). Specify the tie-break for that case and treat it as part of the rule, not an afterthought.

steps.name: Document where the rule can be gamed
steps.description: Every non-dictatorial rule over three or more options can be gamed by some voter in some case (Gibbard-Satterthwaite). Identify which of those tricks your deployment is exposed to. If the agents are strategic, this is a live risk.

steps.name: Audit ballots and outcomes
steps.description: Log every ballot and every count. Re-run the rule offline to confirm the result. Different rules can elect different winners on the same ballots, so an audit makes the rule choice visible after the fact.

principles: The voting rule is the design decision. Defaulting to majority vote is choosing without choosing.
Arrow's result says you cannot have every good property at once. Rank them, accept the losses, and write them down.
The collection form and the rule must match. Use rankings for Borda and approvals for approval voting. Converting between forms loses information.
Every non-dictatorial rule can be gamed somewhere (Gibbard-Satterthwaite). Name where yours can.

known_failure_modes.note: Condorcet cycles trigger endless re-voting because no tie-break was specified up front.
When agents share a model family their preferences cluster — the 'vote' becomes a single agent in many disguises.

sources.title: Multiagent Systems (2nd edition, ed. Gerhard Weiss, MIT Press 2013)
sources.chapter: Ch 6 'Computational Social Choice' — Felix Brandt, Vincent Conitzer, and Ulle Endriss — §1 Introduction; §2 Preference Aggregation; §3 Voting; §4 Combinatorial Domains; §5 Fair Division; §6 Conclusion
sources.quote: Computational Social Choice — Felix Brandt, Vincent Conitzer, and Ulle Endriss — 1 Introduction, 2 Preference Aggregation, 3 Voting, 4 Combinatorial Domains, 5 Fair Division, 6 Conclusion

sources.title: Social Choice and Individual Values (Kenneth J. Arrow, Yale University Press, 1951; rev. 1963)
sources.chapter: Ch V 'The General Possibility Theorem for Social Welfare Functions' — proves Arrow's Impossibility Theorem
sources.quote: Arrow, K. J. (1951). Social Choice and Individual Values. New York: Wiley. — establishes Arrow's General Possibility (Impossibility) Theorem: absent restrictions on individual preferences, no social welfare function jointly satisfies Unrestricted Domain, Pareto efficiency, Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives, and Non-Dictatorship.

methodologies.name: Coalition Formation
methodologies.summary: Group agents into teams that create the most value together, then split the reward fairly. First write down how much value each possible team can produce. Then find the grouping that maximises total value. Then divide each team's reward using a payoff rule that matches what you need: fairness, stability, or keeping the most upset member as happy as possible. Common rules are a fair-share split (the Shapley value), a no-defection split (the core), and a minimise-the-worst-complaint split (the nucleolus). This is more than ad-hoc team building. It gives compact ways to record team values, an account of which payoff rules you can actually compute, and the fairness-versus-stability trade-offs each rule makes.
methodologies.intent: Group agents into teams that maximise joint value and split the reward using a payoff rule whose fairness or stability property matches the deployment.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Use this when several agents could pool resources or skills to produce more together than apart, and the hard question is who joins which team and who gets what share. Examples are joint planning, resource pooling, federated computation, and multi-agent task delivery. Don't apply it when agents are interchangeable and any grouping is fine; simple partitioning is enough. One exception: even interchangeable agents may benefit from this when coordinating inside a team is itself costly.

inputs.name: Agent population
inputs.description: The agents that may form teams, with their individual skills and the cost of working together.

inputs.name: Characteristic function
inputs.description: A record of how much value each possible team can produce. When there are many agents, store it compactly instead of listing every team, such as by recording only the synergies between agents.

inputs.name: Solution concept
inputs.description: The chosen rule for splitting a team's reward. Options include a fair-share split (Shapley value), a no-defection split (core), and a split that keeps the most upset member as happy as possible (nucleolus).

outputs.name: Coalition structure
outputs.description: A grouping of all the agents into teams that comes as close as possible to maximising total value.

outputs.name: Payoff division
outputs.description: How each team's value is split among its members under the chosen rule.

outputs.name: Stability or fairness certificate
outputs.description: Evidence that the split meets its rule's promise, such as no team wanting to defect or the fair-share conditions holding. Or a clear note where it does not.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  agents[Agent population + capabilities] --> rep[Choose compact representation of v(S)]
  rep --> gen[Generate coalition structure: maximise sum v(S)]
  gen --> sc{Pick solution concept}
  sc -- fairness --> sh[Shapley value: average marginal contribution]
  sc -- stability --> core{Core non-empty?}
  sc -- always-exists --> nuc[Nucleolus: minimise max dissatisfaction]
  core -- yes --> coreok[Core payoff]
  core -- no --> eps[Fall back to least-core / epsilon-core / nucleolus]
  sh --> div[Compute payoff division]
  coreok --> div
  eps --> div
  nuc --> div
  div --> verify[Verify stability + fairness properties]
  verify --> out[Coalition structure + payoff + certificate]
  out -.value landscape shifts.-> agents

methodologies.example_scenario:
An edge-computing startup runs a federated inference network. Independent operators contribute GPU nodes and split the revenue from serving inference jobs. The hard question is which operators form which serving pools, and how a pool's revenue is divided. Big operators bring raw capacity. Smaller operators may bring rare hardware, such as Hopper cards, or good locations with low latency to a major customer.

Applying the methodology, the platform team treated each subset of operators as a team. Its value was the expected revenue if those operators served jointly. With 14 operators, listing all 2^14 team values was feasible to compute once a week from logs. But they used a more compact representation that recorded just the synergies, such as a 'Hopper card plus EU-West location' bonus. To find the grouping, they ran an anytime branch-and-bound capped at 30 seconds and kept the best grouping found in that budget. For splitting the reward they chose the fair-share rule (Shapley value), because operators cared more about being seen as fairly paid than about strict stability, and the platform's reputation depended on it. They computed the fair shares by Monte Carlo sampling over orderings.

What they learned: re-forming the teams every Monday caught a slow drift. One operator who had been pivotal lost a key customer's traffic and stopped adding much value. The new fair-share split reflected that within a week and avoided a quarter-long dispute. They documented two known violations honestly. The fair-share split was sometimes not defection-proof, meaning a subgroup could in principle do better by breaking off. They accepted that trade-off because fairness optics outweighed strict stability. The hero-agent failure mode showed up once, when a single operator was contributing 60% of the value. They used that signal to recruit backup capacity.
methodologies.deep_dive:
This is computational coalition formation over a cooperative (characteristic-function) game (Elkind, Rahwan & Jennings). A coalitional game is defined by a **characteristic function** v(S) giving the value of each coalition S ⊆ N.

- **Representation**: the full v(S) is 2^n values and infeasible past small n, so use a compact encoding — synergy coalition groups, **marginal-contribution networks (MC-nets)**, or weighted-graph games — that captures structure succinctly.
- **Coalition structure generation (CSG)**: find the partition of N maximising Σ v(S). CSG is **NP-hard** in general; deployments use exact search for small n, **anytime branch-and-bound** (with a quality bound at any cutoff) for medium n, and bounded heuristics for large n.
- **Solution concepts (payoff division)** — each captures a different property:
  - **Shapley value**: pays each agent its *average marginal contribution* over all join orderings. It is the *unique* division satisfying symmetry, dummy, additivity, and efficiency (Shapley 1953). Fair, but **not guaranteed to be in the core**. Exact computation is exponential; approximate by Monte Carlo sampling over orderings.
  - **Core**: the set of divisions where *no sub-coalition can strictly improve by defecting* — the stability concept. The core **may be empty**; when it is, fall back to the **least-core / ε-core** (minimise the maximum defection incentive).
  - **Nucleolus**: lexicographically minimises the maximum coalition dissatisfaction (excess). It **always exists** and is unique, lies in the core when the core is non-empty, but is expensive to compute.
- **Fairness vs. stability is a genuine trade-off**: the Shapley value can sit outside the core (fair but unstable); a core/nucleolus payoff can be less 'fair' by Shapley's axioms but defection-proof. Choose deliberately and certify or document the violated property.
- **Non-stationarity**: v(S) drifts as agents arrive/leave and values move, so schedule re-formation rather than freezing the first structure (else coalitions go stale — the rogue-agent-drift failure mode).

steps.name: Choose how to record team values
steps.description: Listing the value of every possible team takes 2^n entries and is infeasible past a small number of agents. Use a compact form that records just the structure, such as the synergies between agents or a weighted graph, so the size stays manageable.

steps.name: Find the best grouping
steps.description: Find the grouping of agents that maximises total value. This is hard in general. Use exact search for a few agents, anytime branch-and-bound for medium groups, and bounded heuristics for large ones.

steps.name: Pick a payoff-splitting rule
steps.description: Fair-share split (Shapley value): each agent gets its average added value. It is fair but not always defection-proof. No-defection split (core): no subgroup can break off and do better. It is stable but may not exist. Minimise-the-worst-complaint split (nucleolus): it always exists but can be expensive to compute.

steps.name: Compute the split
steps.description: Apply the chosen rule and note its computational cost. For the no-defection split, first check that it exists. If it does not, fall back to a relaxed version, or switch to the minimise-the-worst-complaint split.

steps.name: Verify stability and fairness
steps.description: Stability check: for any agent, could some subgroup give it a strictly better payoff? Fairness check: does the split treat equal contributors equally and give zero-contributors nothing? If you tolerate a violation, write it down. A silent violation destroys trust in the mechanism.

steps.name: Re-plan when agents or values shift
steps.description: Team values do not stay fixed. New agents arrive, skills change, and prices move. Schedule regular re-forming instead of treating the first grouping as permanent.

principles: Record team values compactly. Listing all 2^n team values is rarely feasible.
Choosing a payoff rule is a fairness-versus-stability trade-off. Pick on purpose and document the trade.
Finding the best grouping is hard in general. Bound it with an anytime algorithm.
Teams are not permanent. Re-form them when the value landscape shifts.

known_failure_modes.note: Coalition structure generation runs forever on hard instances when no anytime cap or approximation bound is specified.
Solution concept is computed once, then ignored as the value landscape shifts — coalitions become stale and unfair.
One agent contributes the bulk of every coalition's value; payoff division reflects that, but the system silently bottlenecks on that single agent.

sources.title: Multiagent Systems (2nd edition, ed. Gerhard Weiss, MIT Press 2013)
sources.chapter: Ch 8 'Computational Coalition Formation' — Edith Elkind, Talal Rahwan, and Nicholas R. Jennings — §1 Introduction; §2 Definitions; §3 Solution Concepts; §4 Representation Formalisms; §5 Coalition Structure Generation; §6 Conclusions
sources.quote: Computational Coalition Formation — Edith Elkind, Talal Rahwan, and Nicholas R. Jennings — 1 Introduction, 2 Definitions, 3 Solution Concepts, 4 Representation Formalisms, 5 Coalition Structure Generation, 6 Conclusions

sources.title: A Value for n-Person Games (Lloyd S. Shapley, 1953)
sources.quote: Shapley, L. S. (1953). A Value for n-Person Games. In H. W. Kuhn & A. W. Tucker (Eds.), Contributions to the Theory of Games, Volume II (Annals of Mathematics Studies, 28), pp. 307-317. Princeton University Press. — defines the unique cooperative-game payoff division satisfying symmetry, dummy, additivity, and efficiency axioms.
